headline: Hiroshima Rewrites the Record Book, the Roughnecks Keep Rolling, and Week 13 Sets Up a Collision Course
subheadline: A new season strikeout record, a ruthless Riverside dismantling, and a pair of frustrating ties shaped a Week 12 that clarified exactly who the contenders are heading into the final stretch.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

**Buttville Skidmarks** vs **Canberra Wombats**, 5-5

There is a particular kind of fantasy frustration reserved for a week when you hit 11 home runs, score 33 runs, and still cannot win. That is where the **Buttville Skidmarks** find themselves after a maddening 5-5 tie with the **Canberra Wombats**, a result that feels less like a draw and more like a squandered opportunity on both sides.

The **Skidmarks** dominated the power categories in ways that should have been decisive. Eleven home runs, 33 runs, 25 RBI, and a NSVH mark of 7 that ranked second in the league this week. On paper, that profile wins most matchups. The problem was everything else. The **Skidmarks** were completely shutout in stolen bases, going 0-for-the-week in SB and surrendering those two category wins without a fight. When you can pick up free wins by simply running the bases, leaving them entirely on the table is a strategic failure that compounded into a very costly non-win.

The **Wombats**, for their part, countered with superior pitching efficiency and edged out the RBI battle 29-25, which was just enough to keep pace. Their rotation only threw 37 innings and struck out 33 batters, which is a light workload by any measure, but the efficiency held.

Neither team pulled away from the bottom half of the standings, and that is the real cost of a tie at this stage. The **Skidmarks** sit at 3-6-3. The **Wombats**, at 0-8-4, continue their remarkable winless run through the season. Week 13 brings the **Skidmarks** a matchup with the **Sugar Land Skeeters** that has genuine playoff bubble implications, and they cannot afford to repeat this week's strategic blind spots.

## The Week's Defining Moment

The **Riverside Roughnecks** spent Week 12 looking less like a fantasy baseball team and more like a proof of concept for what happens when elite pitching and elite power converge in the same lineup at the same time.

Jacob Misiorowski's 0.11 WHIP across 9.0 innings is the kind of line that requires a double-take. That is not a rounding error or a small-sample aberration in the abstract: it is a pitcher who faced batters and essentially refused to let them reach base. That performance anchored a **Roughnecks** pitching staff that went 58.0 innings and posted 67 strikeouts, which ranked second in the league this week behind only the extraordinary output of **Hiroshima Radiation**. And that is where the real conversation about Week 12 begins, because Hiroshima's pitching performance was a genuine landmark.

The **Radiation** racked up 92 strikeouts across 88.2 innings, a new season record, the first time any team has crossed that threshold all year. They led the league by a wide margin in both categories, logging 30 more innings than the second-place **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** and 25 more strikeouts than the second-place **Roughnecks**. Tyler Soderstrom contributed 4 HR and 9 RBI, Zack Gelof generated 8 runs, and a rotation that posted 4 quality starts made the case that this is the most pitching-forward contender in the league.

Back in the **Roughnecks** matchup, Jackson Chourio delivered 5 home runs and 10 RBI and Nick Kurtz added 4 HR and 7 RBI in a 7-2 demolition of **General Hospital** that was never particularly close. When your best hitters are doing that and your starter is posting a 0.11 WHIP, the final score is almost beside the point. The point is the profile, and the **Roughnecks** profile right now is the most complete in this league.

Which makes the Week 13 matchup between **Riverside** and **Hiroshima** the obvious marquee event of the entire season. The most complete team against the most dangerous pitching staff. Whoever wins will make a compelling case for being the team to beat.

## Matchups

### Notable: Albuquerque Bombers def. Sugar Land Skeeters, 8-2

The **Albuquerque Bombers** had already established themselves as a legitimate title contender entering the week. An 8-2 beatdown of the **Sugar Land Skeeters** did nothing to complicate that narrative. Byron Buxton provided 4 home runs to anchor a lineup that also featured Jose Herrera's absurd.607 OBP, and the cumulative effect was a **Bombers** team that led the league in runs (44), RBI (40, tying North Correa's season record from Week 8), and home runs (15) all in the same week. When a team wins the three biggest counting-stat categories simultaneously, the result is rarely competitive.

The more pressing story here belongs to the **Skeeters**, whose rotation posted a 7.28 ERA that ranked last in the league this week. Muncy, Olson, and Tatis showed offensive life, and the **Skeeters** actually led the league with a.390 OBP, but a starting staff that cannot get through innings without damage will eventually overwhelm even a competent lineup. The **Skeeters** face the **Skidmarks** in Week 13, and whether they can stabilize the rotation before that game is a genuine question with playoff implications attached.

### Notable: Wisconsin Rapids Reapers def. Abbotsford-Colby Twins, 5-4

The **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** don't always show up in the headline matchups, which is part of what makes this week worth examining more closely. They won 5-4 over the **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** on the strength of a league-leading 9 NSVH, a 4.01 ERA that ranked second in the league, and contributions from players like Kensuke Okamoto and Andrew Vaughn that signal genuine roster depth rather than one-week variance. For a team sitting at 6-6-0, this was the kind of encouraging, multi-category performance that suggests a sustainable floor. The **Twins**, meanwhile, got a monster week from Alec Burleson (5 HR, 8 RBI), but a 5.22 ERA and the addition of Pallante and Carpenter suggest they know the rotation needs reinforcement. Dropping a 5-4 decision despite that Burleson performance is an outcome that should concern them heading into a Week 13 draw with the **Wombats**.

### Notable: North Correa 5 vs Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs 5

A 5-5 tie between two teams who needed wins more than ties is the kind of result that haunts a playoff race. **North Correa** got 3 home runs and a.542 OBP from Shohei Ohtani and scored 35 runs for the week, but zero quality starts from the rotation is a structural crack that keeps surfacing. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** got an elite Paul Skenes outing (17 K, 2 QS), but only managed 8 HR and 21 RBI from the rest of the roster. Recent waiver additions in Taylor, Ruiz, and Jordan signal urgency, though these look more like depth moves than difference-makers.

### Quick Takes

**Hiroshima Radiation def. Benchwarmers, 7-3.** Sonny Gray's 1.50 ERA and Spencer Arrighetti's 14 strikeouts were legitimately good performances, but a.278 OBP and only 20 runs scored meant the **Benchwarmers** were never going to win enough categories against a team posting 92 strikeouts and 4 quality starts. The structural offensive weakness is real.

**Athletic Park Dragons def. BaseballPro77, 7-3.** James Wood's 3-HR, 9-run, 3-SB monster week and Cristopher Sanchez's 13-K outing gave the **Dragons** enough firepower to pull away comfortably. **BaseballPro77** led the league in stolen bases with 10, which shows genuine category discipline, but losing the power and pitching battles made those SBs insufficient.

**Riverside Roughnecks def. General Hospital, 7-2.** The **Roughnecks** were covered extensively in the Defining Moment section above, but worth noting here: **General Hospital** actually posted the lowest ERA in the league this week at 3.72, and it still only produced a 7-2 loss. When your pitching is that good and you still get blown out, the offense (25 runs scored, a.280 OBP) is the diagnosis.

## Standings Watch

Two weeks ago the standings picture still had some genuine ambiguity at the top. That is starting to close. The **Riverside Roughnecks** at 9-2-1 have separated themselves into a tier of one, their points total of 69 reflecting consistent dominance across categories that does not look like a hot streak so much as a roster built to sustain this level. The **Albuquerque Bombers** at 8-3-1 are the clearest challenger, and their construction, deep offense, reliable pitching, and multi-category balance, makes them the team most capable of catching **Riverside** if the **Roughnecks** stumble.

The **Athletic Park Dragons** (8-3-1) and **BaseballPro77** (8-4-0) are tied in wins and fighting over third place. The **Dragons'** luck delta remains inflated, which is worth monitoring, but their talent is genuine. **BaseballPro77** has been a schedule beneficiary all season (cumulative luck delta of +1.27) and faces a real test in Week 13 against the ascending **Reapers**.

The middle of the standings is genuinely complicated. **Sugar Land Skeeters** at 6-5-1 are fifth, but their ERA crisis threatens to drag them backward. The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** and **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** are both 6-6-0 and pointed in opposite directions: the **Twins** trending down after a razor-thin loss, the **Reapers** trending up after their best week of the season.

**Hiroshima Radiation** is perhaps the most interesting case in the league. At 5-5-2, they are sitting eighth despite what has been a genuinely elite pitching operation all season. A cumulative luck delta of -1.08 means the wins have not come as easily as the performances have deserved. If the Week 13 matchup with the **Roughnecks** goes their way, their record will look much more representative of their talent.

At the bottom, the **Canberra Wombats** remain winless at 0-8-4. Twelve weeks in, that record has moved past "unlucky stretch" territory into a structural question about roster construction.

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky Team: Athletic Park Dragons.** The **Dragons** won 7-3 over **BaseballPro77** and have legitimate talent on the roster; James Wood's 3-HR, 9-run week and Cristopher Sanchez's 13-K performance were real. But the broader picture is worth noting: the **Dragons'** cumulative luck delta sits at +2.12, one of the highest in the league, meaning their overall record has consistently benefited from favorable category variance beyond what their raw production would predict. That is not a critique of their roster, which is genuinely strong. It is a caution against extrapolating too aggressively from a record that has had some structural assistance.

**Unlucky Team: Hiroshima Radiation.** Setting a season-record 92 strikeouts, throwing 88.2 innings, posting 4 quality starts, and still walking away with only a 7-3 win (against a team whose offense was limited to 20 runs and a.278 OBP) is not what misfortune looks like in a single week. But the cumulative picture tells the real story. **Hiroshima's** luck delta sits at -1.08, reflecting a season-long pattern in which elite production has not translated into wins at the expected rate. They are a better team than eighth place suggests, and Week 13 against the **Roughnecks** is their best chance yet to correct that narrative.

## Record Alert

Two records were touched this week, each worth marking. **Hiroshima Radiation** set a new season high for team strikeouts in a single week with 92 K across 88.2 innings, the first time any team has crossed that threshold all season. This is not a tie with a prior benchmark but a new one entirely. For context, their nearest competition this week was the **Roughnecks** with 67 strikeouts; the gap between first and second was larger than the second-place total itself. Separately, the **Albuquerque Bombers** matched the season record for most RBI in a week with 40, tying the mark set by **North Correa** back in Week 8. The **Bombers** reached that number while also leading the league in runs and home runs, which makes the performance worth noting beyond the record itself.

## Waiver Wire

There were no notable waiver wire moves this week across the league.

## Trade Value Watch

**Jacob Misiorowski (Riverside Roughnecks): Buy.** A 0.11 WHIP across 9.0 innings is the kind of line that will generate trade interest in a hurry, but one dominant outing does not establish a trend on its own. The underlying pitch profile and swing-and-miss ability suggest there is real talent here rather than pure sample-size luck, and the **Roughnecks** clearly trust him in meaningful spots. If you can acquire him before the league reacts to this week's performance, the buy window may still be open.

**Byron Buxton (Albuquerque Bombers): Hold.** Buxton delivered 4 home runs this week as part of a **Bombers** lineup that led the league in HR, runs, and RBI. The power is real and has been a consistent asset when he is healthy. The hold designation reflects the familiar qualifier attached to every Buxton evaluation: the talent is elite and the injury history is chronic. If he is healthy, hold aggressively. If any red flags emerge, the sell window should not stay open long.

**Jackson Chourio (Riverside Roughnecks): Buy.** Five home runs and 10 RBI in a single week is a legitimate breakout performance rather than a fluky one for a player with this kind of athleticism and bat speed. The **Roughnecks** clearly have him deployed in a favorable lineup spot, and he is young enough that this could represent a sustained step forward rather than a hot week. Buy before the price reflects what he showed this week.

## Power Rankings

1. **Riverside Roughnecks** | Misiorowski's 0.11 WHIP, Chourio's 5-HR/10-RBI explosion, and a 7-2 demolition of General Hospital make this the most complete team in the league; week 13 vs. Hiroshima is a true title-contender clash | [UP]

2. **Albuquerque Bombers** | An 8-2 beatdown fueled by Buxton's 4 HR, Herrera's.607 OBP, and a balanced pitching line confirms this roster is elite top-to-bottom; they tied the season RBI record and face General Hospital in week 13, a winnable tuneup | [SAME]

3. **Hiroshima Radiation** | Setting a new season strikeout record with 92 K while winning 7-3 is a statement week; Soderstrom (4 HR, 9 RBI), Gelof (8 R), and a three-man rotation that posted 4 QS make this the most dangerous pitching-forward team in the league | [UP]

4. **Athletic Park Dragons** | James Wood's 3-HR, 9-run, 3-SB monster week and Cristopher Sanchez's 13-K outing powered a 7-3 win; the luck index remains inflated but the talent is undeniable heading into a tough North Correa matchup | [SAME]

5. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | Losing 8-2 to the Bombers is an ugly result, but the offense still showed life with Muncy, Olson, and Tatis contributing; the rotation's 7.28 ERA is a genuine crisis that must be addressed before the Skidmarks matchup | [DOWN]

6. **North Correa** | A 5-5 tie is a disappointing result for a roster led by Ohtani (.542 OBP, 3 HR) and a lineup loaded with on-base threats; zero quality starts all week is the glaring rot underneath, and week 13 vs. the Dragons is a brutal draw | [SAME]

7. **BaseballPro77** | A 7-3 loss to the Dragons stings but Kirby, Suarez, and Witt Jr. showed flashes; the cumulative luck delta of +1.27 means the schedule has been a friend, and a week 13 date with Wisconsin Rapids will reveal how real this team is | [DOWN]

8. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Winning 5-4 over the Twins on the strength of NSVH (9), solid pitching, and surprising depth from Okamoto and Andrew Vaughn makes this week the most encouraging performance of their season; more on this team below | [UP]

9. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Alec Burleson's 5 HR and 8 RBI was a genuine standout performance but a 5.22 ERA and a razor-thin 5-4 loss shows the rotation remains the team's Achilles heel; adding Pallante and Carpenter signals an effort to patch the holes | [DOWN]

10. **General Hospital** | Getting steamrolled 7-2 by the Roughnecks despite decent pitching (3.72 ERA) exposes an offense that simply cannot generate enough runs, only 25 scored all week, and adding Kolek and Montgomery suggests a roster rethink is underway | [SAME]

11. **Buttville Skidmarks** | A 5-5 tie with the Wombats when you score 33 runs and go yard 11 times should be a win; zero stolen bases cost them two category wins and dropping Vientos without a quality replacement thins an already shallow bench | [SAME]

12. **Benchwarmers** | Sonny Gray's 1.50 ERA gem and Spencer Arrighetti's 14 Ks were genuinely excellent, but a flat offense (only 20 runs,.278 OBP) handed Hiroshima a 7-3 win; structural offensive weakness continues to cap this team's ceiling | [SAME]

13. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | Paul Skenes delivered 17 K and 2 QS, a legitimately elite pitching performance, but the offense mustered only 8 HR and 21 RBI in a 5-5 tie; adding Taylor, Ruiz, and Jordan signals urgency but these are depth moves, not difference-makers | [UP]

14. **Canberra Wombats** | Jo Adell's 8 runs and Henderson's contributions weren't enough to push past a 5-5 tie, and with only 37 IP and 33 K from the pitching staff they remain dependent on opponents making mistakes; the -1.96 cumulative luck delta is the worst in the league | [DOWN]

## Looking Ahead

The obvious centerpiece of Week 13 is **Hiroshima Radiation** vs **Riverside Roughnecks**: the season's new strikeout record holder against the most complete roster in the league. Both teams are trending upward, both have genuine title aspirations, and the loser will face a difficult path in the final weeks. It is the matchup the whole league should be watching.

Beyond that marquee collision, **Athletic Park Dragons** vs **North Correa** carries significant weight in the middle of the standings. The **Dragons** have been quietly dominant but carry an inflated luck delta; the **Correa** squad has Ohtani and a dangerous lineup but a rotation that posted zero quality starts this week. Whoever wins has a real claim on a top-four seed. Finally, **BaseballPro77** vs **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** functions as a legitimacy test for both sides. The **Reapers** just posted their best week of the season; the **77** have been schedule-aided all year. Week 13 will separate the real from the fortunate.
